fields.description:
## Context
DSO plugin developers and AI agents working on the codebase face increasing difficulty reviewing, testing, and reasoning about core infrastructure scripts. Three Python functions exceed 300 lines: bridge-outbound.py::process_outbound (629 lines), bridge-inbound.py::process_inbound (465 lines), ticket-reducer.py::reduce_ticket (353 lines). Seven files exceed 1000 lines: merge-to-main.sh (1,549), validate.sh (1,328), analyze-tool-use.py (1,225), ticket-graph.py (1,152), bridge-inbound.py (1,148), bridge-outbound.py (1,053), validate-review-output.sh (1,050). Five shell scripts have deep nesting (6+ levels): validate-review-output.sh (max 7, 280 lines), merge-to-main.sh (max 6, 124 lines), validate.sh (max 6, 118 lines), worktree-cleanup.sh (92 lines), sprint-next-batch.sh (75 lines). Additionally, 12 Python functions in the 100-180 line range should be decomposed. This epic does NOT include TODO/FIXME remediation or REVIEW-DEFENSE annotation work (owned by epic 5018-64ce).

## Success Criteria
- No Python function exceeds 180 lines
- No source file (Python or shell) exceeds 1000 lines
- No shell script nesting exceeds 6 levels (24 spaces)
- Extracted handlers organized into domain-grouped subpackages: bridge handlers to scripts/bridge/, ticket processors to scripts/ticket/, shell utilities to hooks/lib/lib-*.sh
- All existing tests pass without modification to test assertions; consumer import paths remain resolvable via re-export shims in the original entry point files or are updated in the same commit with no functional change to callers
- .test-index entries are created for all new handler modules before epic closure

## Scenario Analysis
- importlib path resolution breaks when handlers move (critical, deployment): bridge scripts resolve ticket-reducer.py via Path(__file__).parent. Original entry points must remain as dispatchers preserving sibling resolution.
- Guard clause flattening changes exception propagation (high, deployment): Nested try/except at different levels catch specific types. Flattening must audit and replicate catch semantics.
- New handler modules lack test-index coverage (medium, configuration): Must generate .test-index entries after extraction to avoid full-suite triggers.
- Module re-execution via importlib (medium, runtime): New handler modules should use standard imports, not importlib.

## Approach
Domain Module Reorganization: decompose monster functions into handler modules organized by domain (bridge/, ticket/), extract shell utility functions into lib-*.sh files, flatten deep nesting via guard clauses. Original script entry points remain as thin dispatchers that re-export all public symbols, preserving backward compatibility for 70+ consumers. Guard clause flattening must audit and preserve exception propagation semantics. New handler modules use standard Python package imports (not importlib).

## Dependencies
This epic should complete before epic 9d88-68ed (observability) begins work on bridge scripts, to avoid concurrent structural changes to the same files.
